1920s: The 1920s was a period of massive growth and innovation in music, witnessing the first electrical recordings, the explosion of jazz music, the proliferation of gramophone players, the beginnings of music radio, and even the advent of the BBC.
1930s: As the 1920s wore into the 1930s, jazz continued to dominate the music scene, airing on BBC radio programmes and enjoying print coverage in Melody Maker magazine, which had launched in 1926. During this time, fashions steadily shifted away from the rougher, New Orleans style 'hot' jazz towards smoother, more highly arranged styles.
1940s: In the 1940s, Jazz and Blues were the most popular genres of the time and was also known as part of the 'Swing Era.'  The '40s is also the decade when some of the most classic holiday songs came out including White Christmas by Bing Crosby, and The Christmas Song by Nat King Cole.
1950s: The defining sound of the emergent youth culture during this period was rock and roll – a style of music that both clearly drew upon the popular styles of the previous few decades and at the same time sounded completely fresh and new.
1960s: At the beginning of the 1960s, pop and rock 'n roll trends from the 1950s continued before developing into a more electric style. By the end of the decade, a folk-influenced style of rock music dominated the scene, featuring singer-songwriters such as Bob Dylan, Willie Nelson and Joni Mitchell.
1970s: From the groundbreaking psychedelic rock of Pink Floyd to the disco fever of Donna Summer, 70s music was bustling with diversity and innovation, laying the foundation for some of today's musical styles as a timeless inspiration to the contemporary musical landscape of our generation.
1980s: The 80s marked a vibrant and revolutionary era in music that shaped the musical landscape of its time, and continues to echo through the sounds of contemporary artists.The use of synthesizers and electronic instruments gained popularity during this time, birthing the era of a new wave of musical exploration.
1990s: The 90s music scene was diverse, ranging from grunge and alternative rock to hip-hop, R&B, and pop. Each genre contributed significantly to today's musical landscape, leaving behind a legacy that millennials eagerly revisit.
2000s: The 2000s marked the digital revolution in music. Pop-punk, emo, and R&B dominated charts. The rise of the internet and file sharing changed how music was distributed and consumed.
2010s: The 2010s saw streaming services transform the music industry. EDM, trap, and indie pop gained massive popularity. This decade had one of the highest track counts, reflecting the democratization of music production.
2020s: The 2020s continue the streaming era with diverse genres coexisting. TikTok influences music trends, while artists blend genres more freely. The decade is still unfolding, showing the ongoing evolution of musical expression.
